target: data/sections.json -> part5.html -> the closing sentence of the paragraph inside Section 5.0 that ends with '...The rule was strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test — which is the opposite of what a falsification rule is supposed to do.' Split the single sentence that conflates two critiques into a dedicated follow-on paragraph that separates (1) the upgrade-as-epistemic-standard credit from (2) the retroactive-application / genre-change critique, and adds a Popper reference per SEC-5.0.c1 stronger_arguments[1].
patch_instructions: In data/sections.json, locate the part5.html string. Find the single occurrence of the old_text below (the closing fragment of the penultimate paragraph in Section 5.0's 'The new rule is a weaker rule' subsection). Replace it with new_text. The replacement (a) closes the existing paragraph after 'Refuted by Data.' — dropping the problematic summary sentence — and (b) opens a new paragraph that separates the two critiques cleanly and adds the Popper reference recommended by the c1 reviewer. No other part of part5.html is modified. The old_text fragment occurs exactly once in part5.html (verified at expansion-write time).
old_text: or "Refuted by Data." The rule was strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test — which is the opposite of what a falsification rule is supposed to do.
new_text:
or "Refuted by Data."

Two distinct critiques, kept separate. As a general epistemic standard, the replacement rule is tighter than the old binary-knockout claim. “Genuinely pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, and not already covered by a competing explanation” is harder to satisfy than “any single row confirmed = globe falsified”, and if the author applied this tighter rule consistently to new prediction registrations going forward, that would be a methodological upgrade and we would credit it. As applied to this specific page, however, swapping in the tighter rule after several of the originally-specified rows (Sydney-Perth, Polaris) failed to produce the promised knockout is a different move: it exits the falsification-instrument genre the page previously claimed — a rule that commits in advance that these specific rows, if they fall the wrong way, kill the globe model — and substitutes a methodology-of-good-evidence genre, a survey of how strong the evidence is on the same rows. Both genres are legitimate writing, but only the first is a kill-shot page, and the retroactive substitution runs afoul of Popper’s requirement (K. Popper, The Logic of Scientific Discovery, 1959, ch. 4) that a falsification criterion be specifiable before the test, not retrofitted around its outcome. What the April 17 edit documents is not the rule getting better; it is a change of genre in the page’s rhetorical commitment.
holes_addressed: SEC-5.0.c1 holes_found[3] (moderate, ISS-1217) — framing asymmetry: the single closing sentence 'The rule was strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test — which is the opposite of what a falsification rule is supposed to do' conflates two distinct critiques (epistemic-standard upgrade vs retroactive-application / genre change) into a rhetorical flourish that a careful dome defender can plausibly read as punishing the author for adopting more rigour. The replacement paragraph separates the two critiques explicitly: (i) credits the new rule as tighter at the general-methodology level; (ii) isolates the narrower critique about applying the tighter rule retroactively, AFTER the originally-specified rows failed, as a genre change from falsification instrument to methodology-of-good-evidence. This removes the surface vulnerability while preserving (and strengthening) the substantive argument.
new_evidence_added: Explicit citation of Popper 1959 (The Logic of Scientific Discovery, ch. 4) for the requirement that a falsification criterion be specifiable before the test, not retrofitted around its outcome. This implements SEC-5.0.c1 stronger_arguments[1] — hardens the methodological critique so it cannot be dismissed as our personal taste in rules. Also links to the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy entry on Popper for readers who want the primary concept.

Explicit naming of the two rows (Sydney-Perth, Polaris) that failed to produce the promised knockout before the rule was replaced — anchors the retroactive-application critique in concrete dates/rows rather than letting it float abstractly.

anticipated_objections.objection: You explicitly credit the tighter rule as a methodological upgrade, then attack its use on this page. A dome defender will say: if the rule is better, let it be better — stop penalising good-faith corrections.
anticipated_objections.response: The credit is restricted to the rule as a general statement applied prospectively to new registrations. The critique is about the rule's retroactive application to rows that were already in the falsification-instrument commitment. Those two scopes are orthogonal: one is policy-for-future, the other is reassignment-of-past. We are not penalising the author for adopting rigour; we are noting that swapping falsification rules mid-stream, after specific commitments on specific rows, changes what the page is — not how rigorous its rule is.

anticipated_objections.objection: The genre-change framing (falsification instrument → methodology-of-good-evidence note) is itself a rhetorical move. The author may have always intended the page as guidance for good evidence, and the binary-knockout wording was a drafting error he has now corrected.
anticipated_objections.response: The old rule was framed as an explicit binary commitment and the page was organised as such — individual-row CONFIRMED (globe falsified) labels, a Falsification Rule heading. The change is not a drafting fix; the heading itself was rewritten (to "How To Read This Page Correctly") and the rule body replaced. That is a genre edit, not a wording edit. If the methodology-of-good-evidence framing had always been the intent, the page would not have carried explicit binary-knockout labels on rows that have now been demoted from confirming-globe-falsification to structural-support.

anticipated_objections.objection: The Popper citation is a sledgehammer; other philosophers of science have argued that falsification criteria can legitimately be updated as new evidence arrives (Lakatos 1970).
anticipated_objections.response: Lakatos's research-programme framework does allow for updating — but the updating operates at the research-programme level (protective-belt adjustments), not at the level of specific already-issued falsification claims against specific named rows. Replacing a pre-committed row-level kill-shot with a general methodology statement after the row failed is not a Lakatosian protective-belt move; it is simply withdrawing the falsification commitment on that row. The Popper invocation is specifically about pre-vs-post test specifiability for a commitment that was already made; it is the correct tool for this particular move.

kernel_of_truth: The c1 reviewer is correct that the current one-sentence framing reads as punishing the author for adopting a tighter rule. The replacement rule IS, evaluated on its own terms as a general methodology statement, tighter than the old binary-knockout claim — and a review that silently collapses that fact into a rhetorical flourish is rhetorically weaker than one that explicitly credits the upgrade and then narrows to the specific failure mode (retroactive application to already-committed rows). The split also aligns with the kernel-of-truth standard: acknowledge what is genuinely right first, then show why it does not save the specific move on this specific page.

unaddressed_holes_note: This expansion addresses ONLY SEC-5.0.c1 holes_found[3] (moderate, ISS-1217). The other SEC-5.0.c1 holes remain open: holes_found[0] (major, commit-timestamp accuracy for 5021eec vs 6e06efb — needs api.github.com check); holes_found[1] (major, row-counting ambiguity 2+3+1+1 reads as seven rows on a six-row page); holes_found[2] (moderate, #part5 bare-anchor cross-references do not navigate to per-test blocks); holes_found[4] (minor, "precisely the two post-window rows" TLDR is slightly imprecise for SAA and JFK-LHR); holes_found[5] (minor, 2026-04-18 verification date could be refreshed); holes_found[6] (minor, Timestamp Error tab href target needs an anchor id in EXP-189's patched block); holes_found[7] (minor, ks-claimed CSS rule audit for demoted rows). Each should be tracked under a separate expansion / issue.

tldr_amendment.note: No change to the Section 5.0 ps-tldr is required. The TLDR already leads with "Credit where due" and captures the dated-record framing; the split-critique substance lives one level of disclosure down inside the details, which is the correct place for it. The replacement paragraph strengthens the detail without shifting the TLDR's punchline.
